questions.question: Explain the DNS resolution process with a neat diagram.
keyPoints: Client sends query to recursive resolver
Resolver queries root server
Root server returns TLD server reference
Resolver queries TLD server
TLD server returns authoritative server reference
Resolver queries authoritative server
Authoritative server returns IP address
Resolver caches result and returns to client

questions.question: List and explain the function of any four DNS resource record types.
keyPoints: A Record: Maps domain to IPv4 address
AAAA Record: Maps domain to IPv6 address
MX Record: Specifies mail servers for domain
CNAME Record: Creates alias for another domain name
NS Record: Delegates authority to name servers
SOA Record: Contains zone administration information
PTR Record: Used for reverse DNS lookup

questions.question: Differentiate between recursive and iterative DNS queries.
keyPoints: Recursive: Server performs complete resolution and returns final answer
Iterative: Server returns best answer or referral to next server
Recursive reduces client complexity but increases server load
Iterative is faster and more resilient but requires client to handle referrals
DNS resolvers typically use recursive, root servers use iterative

questions.question: What is DNS caching? Explain the concept of TTL.
keyPoints: Caching stores DNS query results temporarily
Reduces latency by avoiding repeated queries
Decreases load on authoritative DNS servers
TTL (Time To Live) specifies cache duration in seconds
Higher TTL reduces server load but increases stale data risk
Lower TTL provides faster updates but increases server queries
Typical TTL values range from 300 seconds to 86400 seconds

questions.question: Explain the working of Reverse DNS Lookup.
keyPoints: Reverse DNS resolves IP address to domain name
Uses in-addr.arpa zone for IPv4
Uses ip6.arpa zone for IPv6
IPv4: IP 192.168.1.1 becomes 1.1.168.192.in-addr.arpa
PTR records store the mapping
Used by email servers to verify sender authenticity
Returns FQDN if record exists, NXDOMAIN if not

questions.question: Describe the hierarchical structure of DNS with a diagram. Explain the types of DNS servers in this hierarchy.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Root Server → TLD Server → Authoritative Server → Recursive Resolver → Client
Root DNS servers: Top of hierarchy, 13 logical servers (A-M), handle .com, .org, etc.
TLD Servers: Manage top-level domains like .com, .org, .edu, country codes
Authoritative Servers: Hold actual DNS records, either primary or secondary
Recursive Resolvers: Client-facing servers that perform lookups
Caching-only servers: Store temporary results for performance
Stub resolvers: Lightweight clients that query recursive resolvers
Each level delegates to the next, creating distributed architecture

questions.question: Explain DNS security threats and the measures to prevent them.
keyPoints: DNS Cache Poisoning: Attacker inserts false records into resolver cache
DNS Spoofing: Redirecting users to malicious sites
DNS Amplification: Using DNS to amplify DDoS attacks
DNSSEC: Adds cryptographic signatures to verify authenticity
TSIG: Transaction Signature for zone transfers
DNS over HTTPS (DoH): Encrypts DNS queries
DNS over TLS (DoT): Secure transport layer
Rate limiting: Prevents amplification attacks
Regular monitoring and updates
Split-horizon DNS: Different views for internal/external networks

questions.question: Explain the DNS message format and the fields in a DNS header.
keyPoints: DNS uses UDP/TCP on port 53
Header contains: Transaction ID, Flags, Counts
Flags include: QR (query/response), Opcode, AA (authoritative), TC (truncated), RD (recursion desired), RA (recursion available), Response code
Question section: Name, Type, Class
Answer section: Resource records from query
Authority section: Pointers to authoritative servers
Additional section: Extra helpful information
Resource Record format: Name, Type, Class, TTL, RDLENGTH, RDATA
Maximum UDP message size: 512 bytes (512 with EDNS0 can be larger)
